channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: Missing Someone: The Tender Weight of Absence
item.description:
Grief

Missing Someone: The Tender Weight of Absence

Without memories, we wouldn’t experience longing or grief.

Posted March 6, 2026

Key points
  • Recalling a positive emotional memory often leads to the quick emergence of a negative emotion.
  • Distress and longing contain the hope of reunification.
  • The experience of loss often involves regret.
Source: Soloviiiryna/Shutterstock

Throughout history, the emotional hunger of separation has been depicted in literature, theater, and music. In English, an intense longing or grief for something lost or missing is called “desiderium.” In Portuguese and Galician, the word “saudade” describes the feeling of an absence, an “intimate feeling and mood caused by the longing for something absent that is being missed” (Saudade, 2021).

Without memories, we wouldn’t experience longing or grief. Sensory memory may bring to mind a loved one’s scent, the comfort of their touch, or the sound of their voice. Autobiographical memory offers knowledge of the self in the past and helps us imagine that self in the future (A. Baddeley, 1988; Bluck, 2003). These memories remind us of joyfully shared moments—a special restaurant or a funny time together. As we think about what once was and cannot be restored now, a sense of loss may create the heaviness of distress, a sigh of longing.

We tend to believe that recalling a pleasant personal experience will lift a bad mood, and that remembering a distressing personal episode will make it worse. However, a contrast effect can also happen (Wilson & Ross, 2003). In cases of loss, recalling a positive emotional memory often leads to the quick emergence of a negative emotion. Consequently, we automatically reframe the situation based on our current circumstances. As a result, when we face loss, we feel nostalgia and bittersweet feelings, which researchers call “ambivalent affect” (Vaccaro et al., 2020).

A similar mixed positive and negative feeling was observed in addiction cravings (Veilleux et al., 2013). Ambivalent affect is a common experience, though people describe these feelings differently across individuals, cultures, and contexts (Vaccaro et al., 2020). Studies on the concept of love in the United States, Italy, and China revealed that it encompasses both shared and distinct meanings. There are synonyms for “love” in English, but only Chinese has terms like “sad love” and “sorrow love” (Rothbaum & Tsang, 1998).

In times of loss, how do we deal with our feelings? Over time, we develop the ability to tolerate emotions tied to loss. Though memories can be bittersweet, we might find comfort in focusing on what we once had instead of longing to recreate what cannot be brought back now. Understanding that our distress drives us to seek what we lost, we may feel some relief if we can enjoy the happiness we once knew. The Japanese word “natsukashii” describes a nostalgic longing that involves joy and gratitude for the past, rather than a wish to go back to it. However, the initial feeling triggered by reminders of a loved one often pushes us to seek a reunion.

The emotions and memories that create the grief of missing someone or something we highly valued in our life inform us of a present need: Distress and longing contain the hope of reunification. There are times when we can take actions to help mend a broken bond, alleviating the effects of separation distress. For example, in daily life with a partner, family member, or friend, we tend to make up after a disagreement—to reconnect. However, if a loved one dies or becomes unreachable, the other’s sorrow over the lack of closure may trigger a longing to reconnect and drive a futile pursuit of reunion.

A personal example is the recent loss of my brother, who spent the past year battling pancreatic cancer. Among his many talents, my brother restored vintage mandolins, violins, violas, cellos, and guitars. He left behind dozens of string instruments that could have been sold but weren’t because he was emotionally attached to them. Instead, he lived on the edge of financial hardship, surrounded by remarkable relics of the past that he had carefully given new life.

I did not know this side of my brother, although I recognized his love for the past. While I focused on the future, my brother most enjoyed reminiscing about the past and reconnecting with memories. After his death, I found hundreds of notes to himself neatly stacked in a desk drawer. My eyes fixed on one that read, “There is something about antiques that freezes time and puts your mind in a better, more beautiful place.”

Many weeks after my brother died, I began opening his cases of musical instruments, awestruck by the beauty of what he had restored. As I held each precious 100-year-old mandolin, I tearfully imagined he could have told me its history and even taught me to play the majestic instrument. I longed to have him near again, regretting what I did not ask and what I did not know. I finally understood the part of him that had collected, restored, and kept. I was filled with regret.

The experience of loss and missing someone is often filled with regret. We might think that if only we had made different choices, things would have turned out better, but that’s not necessarily true. Would another “goodbye” really lessen the pain we feel about a loved one’s departure? Is there anything we could have said or done that is strong enough to free us from remorse? In truth, survivors are often left holding onto the past, the unspoken, and feelings left behind.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Mary C. Lamia Ph.D.

item.title: Filibustering and Delay Strategies That Block Social Justice
item.description:
Politics

Filibustering and Delay Strategies That Block Social Justice

The covert tactics used to slow down or table systemic change.

Posted March 6, 2026

Key points
  • Organizational filibustering refers to strategies that delay and obstruct efforts to pursue social justice.
  • The use of the filibuster in U.S. politics holds applications to obstruction to progress in systems.
  • Covert obstruction is a risk in the climate in many systems where overt bias may be less tolerated.
  • Change agents can work to prevent organizational filibustering and delay tactics that block change.
Source: Lomb on Pixabay

As a clinical psychologist and academic, I have observed many covert attempts to block social justice efforts within the various systems in which I work. I have been asked to write multiple memos or documents in support of a proposal for a diversity-related policy. I have witnessed leadership attempts to table discussion or add additional votes; one example was an organization's name change to make it more inclusive of diverse gender groups. Committees inserted procedures and processes not normally required. These additions can stretch out the process of implementation of diversity strategic plans or multicultural programs for years.

Unsurprisingly, change agents can become battle-fatigued (Smith, 2004) and give up their efforts. They can also become so disheartened that they leave a group or organization altogether. My colleague named this phenomenon filibustering. Hence, we now use the term “organizational filibustering” to refer to strategies that delay and obstruct efforts to pursue social justice in systems (Mizock and White-Eyes, 2026).

We borrow the term filibuster from U.S. politics, which holds several applications to this obstructive process in organizations. The filibuster has a history in the U.S. government; it was used to impede legislation often related to civil rights (Lau, 2021). Former President Barack Obama has referred to filibusters as a relic of the Jim Crow era in politics when it was used to maintain racial segregation and block voting access among historically marginalized groups. With growing party division, filibusters in Congress have increased (Chafetz, 2011). The excessive use of the filibuster has come to represent the legislature's difficulty in performing its function to reach consensus. Instead, filibusters serve as a loophole that subverts democracy rather than upholds it.

There are aspects of the current political climate and institutional culture that have created a context that is vulnerable to organizational filibustering. In most academic and professional settings, it may not be acceptable to be overtly biased against social justice policies; covert tactics are taken to prevent their implementation. Furthermore, enactors of filibustering may not be aware that their strong internal reaction to social justice policies may reflect aversive racism (Gaertner and colleagues, 2005) and other forms of unconscious bias.

Change agents can be savvy about delay tactics and organizational filibustering to impede progress. Some strategies could include working with leaders who are strong in social justice and can root out hidden acts of obstruction and prevent them. Educating an organization on filibustering and establishing ground rules beforehand can prevent it. Navigating these problems takes strong interpersonal skills, group facilitation, alliance-building, and relationship repair in the face of conflict. Ultimately, we need to raise awareness within ourselves and our organizations to prevent obstruction of social justice from taking hold.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Lauren Mizock Ph.D.

item.title: The Observer Effect in Everyday Life
item.description:
Identity

The Observer Effect in Everyday Life

How daily reflection creates identity, choice, and reality.

Posted March 6, 2026

Key points
  • You may be more like a quantum observer than you think.
  • Repeated choice rewires our brains and makes us who we are.
  • When we track our behaviors, that behavior changes to reflect what we're tracking.
  • You become who you are not as a result of a single, dramatic decision, but by small, consistent choices.

What if you woke up tomorrow and realized that who you have become isn’t only the result of your habits and intentions, but also of how closely you observed yourself today? Psychology explains that who we identify as is the result of repetition. The things we do over and over again become, in a sense, who we are. In our brains, neural pathways are established over time through repeated use. Also, over time, our behaviors become automatic. We may even cease to be aware of our actions and instead perform them out of rote repetition. What this describes is neuroplasticity: the well-documented mechanism by which repeated choices imprint on the brain, and the brain, in turn, shapes the self. Before the brain gets to neuroplasticity, though, something else is at work. And it has less to do with habit formation and more to do with the simple act of noticing. It’s awareness.

The Psychology of Repeated Choice

In behavioral science, identity follows action. If you’re generous, you’ll begin to see yourself as generous. If you're a patient person, you’ll come to see that as part of who you are. Over time, the brain will wire itself to repeat these patterns. This is why a simple practice called daily reflection is so powerful. When you consistently reflect on your behavior each day, you become more like the behaviors on which you’re reflecting. In our lives, daily reflection might be practiced by asking yourself questions like these at the end of each day:

  • Did I do my best to set clear goals?
  • Did I do my best to make progress?
  • Did I do my best to build positive relationships?
  • Did I do my best to find meaning?

When you do this, you’re not just reviewing what you did that day. You’re observing who you are, and then becoming it. This mechanism has its roots not only in behavior but also in physics.

Source: Image by Stadnik from Pixabay

The Observer Effect in Everyday Life

In quantum physics, a principle called the observer effect explains that the simple act of measuring something affects what is being measured. In psychology, that same principle might be at work when we self-reflect. In behavioral science research, studies have consistently shown that when individuals track a behavior, that behavior changes. (1)

Track your steps, and you walk more.
Track your spending, and you spend differently.
Track your effort toward kindness, and kindness increases.

Daily reflection is a way to apply this principle in our everyday lives. It shines a spotlight on the behavior itself. And when behavior is observed consistently, it solidifies into neural pathways in the brain. We start behaving differently, not because someone else is judging us, but because we are measuring ourselves. The simple act of asking ourselves reflective questions each day shapes the behaviors in our lives, which, in turn, make us the people who exhibit those behaviors.

Entanglement: Why Change Is Never Isolated

Another principle from quantum theory, entanglement, might also be at play when we do daily reflection. Quantum entanglement describes how particles can become linked to one another so that a change in one results in a change in the other. In the same way, the effort we make to change in one part of our lives is rarely confined to that part. Instead, our behaviors extend outward and affect those in relationship to us and around us. For example, your attempt to speak in positive terms, rather than negative ones, can influence your colleagues at work. Your intention to control your emotional outbursts can affect your family. Your efforts to build positive relationships at work or in your community can change the dynamics of those relationships. And when you combine these intentions with daily reflection, you’re not only strengthening a positive personal trait within yourself, but also influencing the bigger, interpersonal systems around you.

Consciousness Is Fundamental

Philosophers, physicians, and physicists are forever debating what consciousness is. Is who we are just a byproduct of biology and the brain’s physiology, or is who we are more fundamental and exists irrespective of the brain’s neural firing? We may never know. That said, one thing is true: Conscious awareness shapes who we are. Without reflection, behavior defaults to habit. With reflection, possibility re-enters the system. The practice of asking yourself daily reflective questions puts you in the role of an observer rather than an actor. And from there, you can be intentional about who you choose to be tomorrow.

You don’t become a different person by making a single, dramatic decision. You become a different person by repeatedly observing your daily behaviors and adjusting. In this way, you’re not just developing habits. You’re bringing into reality a chosen, better version of yourself, day after day. As a quantum physicist might put it, identity isn’t a fixed object. It’s an emergent pattern stabilized by consistent attention. So the next time you ask yourself whether you did your best, appreciate what’s really going on. You’re in the role of observer. You’re narrowing possibilities into concrete behavior. And through repeated observation, you are consciously creating the version of yourself that you want to become real. In that sense, the most powerful force for lasting change isn’t intensity. It’s awareness, applied consistently.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Lisa Broderick

item.title: Simple Relationship Tool to Ease Conflict and Grow Closer
item.description:
Relationships

Simple Relationship Tool to Ease Conflict and Grow Closer

It might not be sexy, but calender meetings help your intimacy in the long run.

Posted March 6, 2026

Key points
  • A simple tool of holding a regular calendar meeting can help prevent misunderstandings and resentment.
  • Structure isn't sexy but can create a more "we are a team" environment, which opens all levels of intimacy.
  • If you are someone who needs spontaneity, use your calendar meeting to build in times for flow.
  • Calendar meetings help you create the life and relationship you truly desire.

I see a lot of couples face unnecessary discord because they do not carve out time to discuss the schedule of their lives together. Typically, partners wing it–week by week or day by day. This becomes fertile ground for clashes in communication, concealed expectations, stress, resentment, and a life where fun as a couple gets squeezed out.

There are many facets to modern-day relationships. In my book, A Soulful Marriage, I use a wheel to represent the "8 friendships" that either bring light or darkness to your bond. Think of these 8 friendships as the spokes inside your relationship: 1) Emotional, 2) Physical/Sexual, 3) Domestic, 4) Recreational, 5) Parenting, 6) Community, 7) Financial, and 8) Spiritual.

Like all wheels, the relationship wheel has a tire. In your relationship, communication — effective, open, and soulful communication — is the tire that allows you to safely and effectively move the relationship forward.

"Calendar meetings" are particularly important for your domestic, parenting, and recreational relationships. However, these proactive conversations can touch and enhance each facet of your love and life together, directly and indirectly.

Calendar meetings are highly undervalued by most spouses, and I was one of them. In fact, as someone who falls on the spontaneous versus planner side of the spectrum, I resisted. However, when my husband and I began these sit-down schedule sessions regularly, I became a convert. We both felt more balanced, together, and lighter. This article is to help you appreciate the surprisingly powerful tool of regular "calendar meetings" and how to do them well.

Why Make Time For Calendar Meetings?

  1. To reduce stress and living in reaction to crisis.
  2. To prevent silent resentment, assumptions, and/or feeling unsupported.
  3. To feel "we are a team" with a shared vision, which makes everything lighter and more fun.
  4. To bring dreams and expectations out in the open to be honored and negotiated.
  5. To smooth differences in attachment styles by creating both predictability and space.
  6. To balance the emotional and mental load of day-to-day family life.
  7. To live intentionally and aligned with what matters most in your life and your relationship.
  8. To balance planners and spontaneous partners with structure that still leaves room for flexibility.

Tips for Calendar Meetings That Bring You Closer

  1. Pick a time that works well for you both — e.g., Sunday mornings.
  2. Sit somewhere comfortable — like the couch or kitchen table — and refrain from multi-tasking.
  3. Commit to some level of regularity, e.g., weekly, even if you think there is nothing big to discuss.
  4. Keep it to 20-30 minutes, though consistency is more important than length.
  5. Make it more relational than just logistical, with questions to start off like:
    1. What was one thing you appreciated about our relationship this week?
    2. What is something that would help you feel supported by me this week?
  6. Come with an open heart and intention to support each other and the relationship, especially for potential stress points. This could sound like:
    1. Where do you feel overwhelmed?
    2. How can we lighten the load for each other?
  7. Include "practical matters" such as work events, family travel, children's schedule, family obligations, division of domestic labor, and work needs.
  8. Make sure to include "playful and soulful matters" too, like creating time for just the two of you, spiritual endeavors, entertaining, couple travel, volunteering, birthday wishes, or big picture dreams, needs, and desires.
  9. End with something positive. Consider questions like:
    • What did we handle well together?
    • What is something you are looking forward to?
  • What did we handle well together?
  • What is something you are looking forward to?

In Sum

Calendar meetings aren't enough for a great relationship, but they are a simple tool to remove barriers and create a sense of unity. They are one way to live the "priority pillar" of your relationship, which is what happens when "we make each other the most important other person in our lives."

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Rachel Glik Ed.D., LPC
